item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I find it funny that the documentation on the article is missing!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The original source of the image that was used in this article could not be accessed, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This video is no longer available because the YouTube account associated with this video has been terminated.

cursed comment

item.title: Re: Reminder
item.content:encoded: Then people in comas and the profoundly retarded would fade out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1467 physically assaulted D-9452, who proceeded to break SCP-1467's nose. The subjects had to be restrained by guards. Test aborted. Therapist reports an improvement in SCP-1467's mental state.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R9HKxMgekCo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't (the anomaly) actively erasing proof of his existence, just his physical existence. All previous info pertaining to and around him was wiped all at once in the past, it seems.

Anomaly is just affecting him physically, at this point it isn't actively attacking information of his existence, just his existence itself. And even if he were gone, unless the anomaly does another wiping loop, it's not like they can say he didn't exist.

Proof in the tapes, like if someone died, you'd still have home videos and stuff to remember them by.

Well…..this guy doesn't. Still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea itself is downright fascinating, but the writing needs a lot of work - throughout the article I was frequently confused, as it was often unclear what was meant in some places.

Also, maybe I'm missing something, but if the man's condition causes records of his and his family's existence to disappear from government databases, how do recordings of his voice not get erased?

But yeah, the idea saves this from a downvote for me. No-vote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: No, ectoentropic isn't relevant here because the scip is not violating the laws of physics by bringing materials into being out of nowhere. If anything, entropic would be more applicable, as the scip and objects and persons around it are slowly disappearing. However, there is too little evidence of an actual entropic process (i.e. decay of some sort) in the article to apply that one.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Uhhh… Milo359, no response doesn't mean "go ahead and do it". The only thing it's guaranteed to mean is "no one has addressed this yet".

I'll message some of the people who help out with tags on the site. For future reference, please contact a staff member the next time you have a query like this that is not addressed promptly.

In the meantime, I've removed the new tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see the demon of the second law here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since no one has responded to this post, I'ma just add it :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that the ectoentropic tag should be on this. Permission to add? (The reason that I'm asking is that I'm not sure, & I want the author's feedback on this decision to clarify).

item.title: Reminder
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of one scene in South Park where Kyle had an existential crisis and faded from existence because he ceased to believe in his own existence.

Humans usually remember that they exist…

Perhaps this man may NOT be anomalous, and humans have to be aware that they exist to continue existing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow.
One sure sign of a piece of writing being good is when I don't cringe at all at the writing itself - yeah, Im mad that way. This was the case here. Well done! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Inconsistent use of single and double quotes in this article. Maybe pick one and stick with it? Unless there's a reason I'm missing for using single quotes in the first section, and double quotes after that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap man that is seriously awesome, +1.

Just looked it up, pretty cool origins for the poem. Neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday upon the stair
I met a man who wasn't there
He wasn't there again today
I wish that man would go away

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, this is a really creepy kind of anomaly to possess, and the final experiment only makes it creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why he gotta be black tho?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently the Foundation have found no records of subject's stated wife, children, house, car or extended family ever having existed.

This line really got to me. I imagined his entire family fading out of existence, and only he was able to save himself.

Experiment 19's log is pretty lacking in organization; I've fixed its formatting and cleaned the addendum in general.

item.title: Featured!
item.content:encoded: This one makes me nostalgic, as it's a favorite of mine from when I was binge-viewing the SCP lists back when I first joined the site. An excellent choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I find it very impressive how you make it so vague on how his effect spreads. It makes me wonder just how this guy's effect works, and whether he's a guy who's been forgotten, or a guy who never existed.

+1, because confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Today he's listed as being 47 years old. In 2024 some researcher reads his file, he's still listed as being 47 years old. We are presumed to know when the article was made, but documents such as this one should make no presumptions.

A lot of articles suffer from this, unfortunately.

UPD: Also, a 550K .jpg? Should have made a thumbnail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why it's necessary to give 1467 a fixed date of birth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
47 year old male

This was written 2.5 years ago. In case he actually does age, a year of birth would be more adequate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know about this… I really like the idea, but it seems much too long. Too many tests, and it's all too dense. I can't figure out what's going on, and it didn't hold my attention to the bottom of the page. I want to love this, and I'm sure with a bit of tidying I absolutely would.

That said, it's not poorly-rated, so maybe it's just me having a poor attention span this afternoon.

One thing, though:

all relevant social security numbers remain unassigned due to what appears to be a computer error.

There's no such thing as computer error. Computers don't just make mistakes. It's humans that introduce the errors into the system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I really like the concept behind this article, but I noticed a lot of grammatical errors, so I went and fixed a few basic spelling/grammar things - subject-verb agreement, that sort of thing. However, I still feel like the writing style gets in the way of the content itself at some points. A quick scan reveals that there are rather a lot of adverbs throughout the article, and while there's nothing wrong with that, per se, I feel like it weakens the professional tone and the writing overall here.

I also feel like, while you've explained that English isn't your first language, some of the slightly unusual wording choices take away from the tone and fluidity of the article overall. For example, in the sentence, "Habits such as repeating its name, checking its pulse, keeping itself talking to others, drawing and writing descriptions of itself on its body and surrounding surfaces and keeping recordings of its voice going while sleeping all developed over time," the subject - "habits" - is so far from the verb - "developed" - that, at least for me, the sentence seems a bit cumbersome and difficult to comprehend at first glance. I'm not going to pick through the whole article, but there are various similar small things throughout, which got in the way when I tried to read through it for the first time.

Aside from that, though, the idea here is wonderful, and the actual content is fine. For now, though, I think I'm going to have to give this a -1 just because it felt so unnatural to read through in terms of style and tone. I think a good thorough clean-up would do wonders for this article, though.

Just one last small criticism here, though. I feel like the "refer to the SCP by its designation and stop humanizing it, personnel" addendum has been done before, even if this SCP has the whole twist of needing to be referred to by its name in person. I just don't feel like it adds much at this point given how many other articles have done it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nobody couldnt exist less not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not see the cluttering everybody mentions, but I like the picture in context of the SCP the most. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but dear god, is it ever messy. I can't upvote because of all of the extraneous crap, but it's quite good.

Also, this reminds me of one of Richard Matheson's short stories (the name of which I forget) about a man who gradually fades from existence. Did you read it?

Spoylerz brah spoylerz
After his friends, his wife, his entire life, and any record of his existence have all vanished, the story ends with...

I think I'll get some cof

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 's cool. English isn't my first language and I don't write that often so my grammar is pretty spotty. Thanks for the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fooloftime is still active and posting in other threads. Maybe try to send a PM about it first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …or you could ask the author before making such changes. Because, you know, it's only polite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was waaaaay too good for me to not give it my own Grammar Fix and Format Clean-up Treatment. Fixed some spelling, reworded some awkward sentences and took out a few tiny details that were detracting from the clinical tone… and, of course, upvoted for being an awesome idea :)

I will post my slightly-revised version tomorrow, and I hope everyone likes it — OP, feel free to revert if you think I changed the style too much. I won't be offended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorites. This story gave me chills. (One of my biggest fears is losing sight of reality.) Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the help. I at least put in the extra paragraphs. Considering doing something about the containment procedures also, they're a bit repetetive. Don't want to mess it up though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear god this was still so messy even after other people have gone over it.

Made a bunch of grammar fixes, plus one error correction, which I'm listing here because it's a "gray area" edit that the author might want to change (though christ knows why). And it STILL could use cleanup…

Changed:

There have been no car accident at the given date

To:

There have been no car accidents in the claimed area at the given date

There are significantly over ten thousand car accidents happening in the US alone every single day, so a car accident free day would be an anomalous effect in and of itself.

Overall, I can't decide whether I like this article or not. But I may be biased by all the clean-up I just did, so I'll give it another look another time… and hopefully the author will have done a little more work on it? (And maybe added a few more paragraph breaks to the Description and colons to the log? And… Etc.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
losing oneself in pain… quite literally.
this hits close to home and almost hurt to read at points.
upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: poor ghost dad :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this, I really do, but the mechanism that keeps him from fading just seems too corny to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing here still needs some serious help. Fortunately the concept is solid, if reminiscent of my understanding of Shrodinger from the Hellsing series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha. I tried to clarify it. (Only added a sentence, so as to not mess up any grammar fixes to much.) As for the is to be, I'll try to keep it in mind if I write another SCP ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah missed that part in the single massive paragraph.

SCP-1467 should be contained in a padded cell with no less than three (3) audio recording devices installed in the walls. Batteries are to be changed weekly.

…is where my confusion started, and stuck around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um… I quote: "…and keeping recordings of its voice going while sleeping…"

I am really confused why this is unclear. He keeps a recording of his voice going while sleeping. Seriously, why is this unclear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Okay, fair enough. But I will change the world! Oh, yes! Just you wait…
  • The recorders being battery powered is a temporary patch, not meant for long term.
  • WHY they are there becomes obvious as the article is read, and I have not objections. It just seems that like a better reason to address him by name as (non-canon, etc.) the Foundation doesn't care about addressing SCPs by name unless they tend to kill reseachers (Able, I'm looking at you!)

And he no longer sleeps? Seriously, he couldn't speak for 40 minutes and he was almost gone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed mass grammatical… yuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll respond to each point.

- Seems a minor detail. I can find several SCP using should be on a trivial(10 second) search trough. If it becomes a thing and more people mention it, or if it's just plain bad English I'll change it, otherwise it can stay.
- Yes. Drawing lines of electricity seems like a bother when you can just screw in a few recorders into the wall and call it a day. It's not like putting in a few AA's each week is a horrid strain on resources.
- The audio recording devices are there for a reason. Consider how they bring him back during the experiments and the description of his habits. Maybe I should spell it out clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ARGH!

SCP-1467 should be contained in a padded cell with no less than three (3) audio recording devices installed in the walls.

Containment procedures aren't suggestions. "IS to be" not "SHOULD BE"

Batteries are to be changed weekly.

You've permanently mounted them on walls and you're relying on batteries?

SCP-1467 should always be referred to in person as 'Mr Smith', as referring to it by any other means throws it into a panic/rage state.

Actually, how about "If it not refereed to by it's preferred name, it will begin its fade process."

40 minutes? Does it no longer sleep?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been known to affect objects and personnel in SCP-1467 immediate vicinity.

Currently the Foundation have found no records of subjects stated wife, children, house, car or extended family ever having existed.

I would think that they'd be worried that this might eventually effect the Site it's housed at. If an entire Site was retroactively erased from existence, including all the SCPs it housed and all of the staff working there, it might cause containment breaches at other sites. Or severely screw up the fabric of reality. Or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Added a name. Made an announcment. (Made a new thread so yeah, *facepalm*) and am currently working on formating. The weird wording I'm not sure what to do about. English ain't my first language, As for the extra stuff, I'll see what I can do.

Edit: I added three more experiment logs. Uncertain on whether it adds anything or whether it makes the article unwieldy. Might remove them later but figured I'd give them a try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm remembered, therefore I am. Descartes would be proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this finished? If it is, you should add a name and announce it in the announcement thread.

I like the concept, but it would be nice to see the extent to which 1467's condition can affect others described more clearly. Does the recordings in which he describes himself fade out too? With the wife and children, was the intent that they were erased (postmortem) because they were a part of his life, or because they contracted similar condition? If the former, have it affected anyone else? If we put a D-Class in a cell with 1467 to socialize, then separate them, will D-Class fade out?

Also, is there a reason for administering amnesiacs to 1467 after tests? Because I don't see any.

Wording is fonky in some places, too. And formating of the experiments. 10m:12s should be changed to just 10:12, I think, or to "10 minutes after the start of experiment", with seconds dropped. And there is an established formating for things like interviews and audio transcriptions, using wikidot citation syntax ( "> " in the begining of the line, with space, without quotes; colon after D's designations).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Messy, needs work, but a very very good idea, so it gets my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here goes nothing.
